KARC_01_water_cycle.passage: The sun is the engine of the water cycle. Its heat warms the oceans, lakes, and puddles every day.

KARC_01_water_cycle.passage: High above the ground, water vapor cools down. The vapor turns back into tiny liquid drops. Many drops together form a cloud. This is called CONDENSATION.

KARC_01_water_cycle.answer: it turns back into liquid drops, forming clouds

KARC_01_water_cycle.passage: When too much water has condensed in a cloud, the drops get heavy. They fall as PRECIPITATION — rain, snow, sleet, or hail.

KARC_01_water_cycle.answer: Water gathers in rivers, lakes, oceans, and underground

KARC_01_water_cycle.passage: Once water reaches the ocean or a lake, the cycle can start again. The sun heats the water, and the whole process repeats.
KARC_01_water_cycle.question: After collection, what step happens NEXT in the water cycle?

KARC_01_water_cycle.passage: The water cycle never stops. The water you drink today may have been in a cloud last week and an ocean a year ago.
KARC_01_water_cycle.question: Based on the WHOLE arc, why does the same water keep being used over and over?
choices: Because we make new water every day
Because the water cycle (evaporation, condensation, precipitation, collection) keeps moving water around Earth

KARC_01_water_cycle.answer: Because the water cycle (evaporation, condensation, precipitation, collection) keeps moving water around Earth

KARC_03_body_germs.passage: If a germ does get inside, special cells in your blood called WHITE BLOOD CELLS find and destroy it. Think of them as tiny soldiers in your blood.

KARC_03_body_germs.answer: Remember and quickly fight germs the body has seen before

KARC_03_body_germs.passage: A VACCINE gives the body a safe practice round. It teaches the body to make antibodies against a germ BEFORE the real germ ever shows up. Then, if the real germ comes, the body is ready.
KARC_03_body_germs.question: Why do vaccines help?
choices: They teach the body to make antibodies in advance, so it can fight the real germ fast
KARC_03_body_germs.answer: They teach the body to make antibodies in advance, so it can fight the real germ fast

KARC_03_body_germs.passage: All these defenses — skin, white blood cells, fever, antibodies, vaccines — work together. We call this the IMMUNE SYSTEM.
KARC_03_body_germs.question: Based on the WHOLE arc, what is the immune system?
choices: The body's full set of defenses against germs working together
KARC_03_body_germs.answer: The body's full set of defenses against germs working together

KARC_04_ancient_egypt.passage: The Nile is the longest river in Africa. Ancient Egypt grew up along its banks more than 5,000 years ago. Without the Nile, the desert around it would have been too dry for farming.
KARC_04_ancient_egypt.question: Why was the Nile important to Ancient Egypt?
choices: It made farming possible in a desert area
It blocked enemies from reaching the cities
KARC_04_ancient_egypt.answer: It made farming possible in a desert area

KARC_04_ancient_egypt.passage: Once a year, the Nile FLOODED. The flood spread rich black soil onto the land along the river. After the water went down, farmers planted wheat and barley in this soil.
KARC_04_ancient_egypt.question: Why did the yearly Nile flood help farmers?
choices: It left rich soil that made plants grow well
It was used as a calendar only
KARC_04_ancient_egypt.answer: It left rich soil that made plants grow well

KARC_04_ancient_egypt.passage: Egypt was ruled by kings and queens called PHARAOHS. Egyptians believed the pharaoh was very powerful — a leader of both people and worship.
KARC_04_ancient_egypt.question: Who were the pharaohs?
choices: The kings and queens who ruled Ancient Egypt
KARC_04_ancient_egypt.answer: The kings and queens who ruled Ancient Egypt

KARC_04_ancient_egypt.passage: When a pharaoh died, the Egyptians often built a giant stone tomb called a PYRAMID to bury the pharaoh and their treasures. The largest pyramids are still standing today.
KARC_04_ancient_egypt.question: What is a pyramid?
choices: A kind of boat used on the Nile
A giant stone tomb built for a pharaoh
KARC_04_ancient_egypt.answer: A giant stone tomb built for a pharaoh

KARC_04_ancient_egypt.passage: The Egyptians wrote with picture symbols called HIEROGLYPHS. Each picture stood for a sound, a word, or an idea. Hieroglyphs covered tomb walls and sacred objects.
KARC_04_ancient_egypt.question: What are hieroglyphs?

KARC_04_ancient_egypt.passage: Because hieroglyphs were on the walls of pyramids, scholars later read them and learned about pharaohs, farming along the Nile, and Egyptian beliefs.
KARC_04_ancient_egypt.question: Based on the WHOLE arc, how do we know so much about Ancient Egypt today?
choices: The hieroglyphs they wrote on pyramid walls and tombs taught us about their pharaohs, farming, and beliefs
Ancient Egypt is still ruled by pharaohs
KARC_04_ancient_egypt.answer: The hieroglyphs they wrote on pyramid walls and tombs taught us about their pharaohs, farming, and beliefs

KARC_04_ancient_egypt.passage: Imagine the Nile dried up one year and did not flood. Farmers would have no rich soil.
KARC_04_ancient_egypt.question: Based on the WHOLE arc, what would have most likely happened to the Egyptian civilization if the Nile stopped flooding?
choices: Farming would fail and the civilization would struggle to survive
KARC_04_ancient_egypt.answer: Farming would fail and the civilization would struggle to survive

KARC_05_food_chains.passage: Every food chain starts with the sun. Plants use sunlight to make their own food. Because they make their own food, plants are called PRODUCERS.
KARC_05_food_chains.question: Why are plants called producers?
choices: They make their own food using sunlight.
KARC_05_food_chains.answer: They make their own food using sunlight.

KARC_05_food_chains.passage: Animals cannot make their own food. They must eat plants or other animals to get energy. An animal that eats food is called a CONSUMER.
KARC_05_food_chains.question: What is a consumer?
choices: An animal that eats plants or other animals.
A plant that makes its own food.
The sun that gives off heat.
KARC_05_food_chains.answer: An animal that eats plants or other animals.

KARC_05_food_chains.passage: Consumers that eat only plants are called HERBIVORES. A rabbit that eats grass and a deer that eats leaves are both herbivores.
KARC_05_food_chains.question: Which animal is a herbivore?

KARC_05_food_chains.passage: A consumer that eats other animals is a CARNIVORE. A carnivore that hunts other animals is a PREDATOR, and the animal it hunts is the PREY.
KARC_05_food_chains.question: In a food chain where a fox hunts a mouse, the mouse is the…

KARC_05_food_chains.passage: When plants and animals die, DECOMPOSERS such as mushrooms and worms break them down. This returns nutrients to the soil so producers can grow again.
KARC_05_food_chains.question: What do decomposers do?
choices: They break down dead plants and animals.
They block the sun from plants.
KARC_05_food_chains.answer: They break down dead plants and animals.

KARC_05_food_chains.passage: Energy moves in one direction: from the sun to producers, then to herbivores, then to predators. At each step, some energy is lost as heat.
KARC_05_food_chains.question: Where does the energy in every food chain come from first?

KARC_05_food_chains.passage: Imagine a forest where every decomposer suddenly disappeared. Dead leaves, plants, and animals would no longer be broken down.
KARC_05_food_chains.question: Based on the whole arc, what would most likely happen?
choices: Plants would grow faster than ever.
The sun would stop giving energy.
Dead matter would pile up and the soil would lose its nutrients.
KARC_05_food_chains.answer: Dead matter would pile up and the soil would lose its nutrients.

KARC_06_simple_machines.passage: A SIMPLE MACHINE is a tool with few or no moving parts that makes work easier. In science, WORK means using a force to move an object.
KARC_06_simple_machines.question: What does a simple machine do?
choices: It removes the need for force.

KARC_06_simple_machines.passage: A LEVER is a stiff bar that turns on a fixed point. That fixed point is called the FULCRUM. A seesaw is a lever, and the bar in the middle rests on a fulcrum.
KARC_06_simple_machines.question: The fixed point that a lever turns on is called the…

KARC_06_simple_machines.passage: An INCLINED PLANE is a flat, slanted surface — a ramp. It is easier to push a heavy box up a ramp than to lift the box straight up.
KARC_06_simple_machines.question: Why is an inclined plane helpful?
choices: It turns the load into a wheel.
It makes the load weigh less.
It is easier to push a load up a slant than to lift it straight up.
KARC_06_simple_machines.answer: It is easier to push a load up a slant than to lift it straight up.

KARC_06_simple_machines.passage: A WHEEL AND AXLE is a wheel attached to a rod called an axle. When the axle turns, the wheel turns with it, helping move heavy loads with less force.
KARC_06_simple_machines.question: A wheel and axle helps you…
choices: lift loads straight into the air.
move heavy loads with less force.
KARC_06_simple_machines.answer: move heavy loads with less force.

KARC_06_simple_machines.passage: A PULLEY is a wheel with a rope around it. When you pull down on the rope, the load on the other end goes up. Flagpoles use pulleys to raise flags.
KARC_06_simple_machines.question: What does a pulley help you do?
choices: Lift a load up by pulling down on a rope.
Slide a load along the ground.
KARC_06_simple_machines.answer: Lift a load up by pulling down on a rope.

KARC_06_simple_machines.passage: Levers, ramps, wheels, and pulleys all do the same basic job. They let you move something using less force, even though you may have to move that force a longer distance.
KARC_06_simple_machines.question: What do all simple machines have in common?
choices: They let you do work with less force.
They all have many moving parts.
KARC_06_simple_machines.answer: They let you do work with less force.

KARC_06_simple_machines.passage: A worker needs to load heavy barrels from the ground onto the back of a truck. The barrels are too heavy to lift straight up.
KARC_06_simple_machines.question: Based on the whole arc, which simple machine would help most?
choices: A pulley laid flat on the ground.
A fulcrum with no bar attached.
An inclined plane, to roll the barrels up a ramp.
KARC_06_simple_machines.answer: An inclined plane, to roll the barrels up a ramp.

KARC_07_american_revolution.passage: In the 1700s, the country of Britain ruled thirteen COLONIES along the east coast of North America. The people who lived in the colonies were called COLONISTS.
KARC_07_american_revolution.question: Who ruled the thirteen colonies in the 1700s?

KARC_07_american_revolution.passage: Britain made the colonists pay TAXES — extra money — on everyday things like tea and paper. The colonists grew angry because they had no say in these new tax laws.
KARC_07_american_revolution.question: Why did the colonists grow angry about taxes?
choices: Britain paid the taxes for them.
They had no say in the tax laws they were forced to obey.
The taxes were too small to matter.
They were not allowed to drink tea.
KARC_07_american_revolution.answer: They had no say in the tax laws they were forced to obey.

KARC_07_american_revolution.passage: The colonists believed it was unfair to be taxed by a government they could not vote for. Their famous protest cry became "No taxation without representation."
KARC_07_american_revolution.question: What did "No taxation without representation" mean?
choices: Colonists should never pay for anything.
It is unfair to be taxed by a government you cannot vote for.
KARC_07_american_revolution.answer: It is unfair to be taxed by a government you cannot vote for.

KARC_07_american_revolution.passage: On July 4, 1776, the colonists signed the DECLARATION OF INDEPENDENCE. It announced that the colonies were no longer ruled by Britain — they were a free country, the United States of America.
KARC_07_american_revolution.question: What did the Declaration of Independence announce?
choices: That the war was already over.
That the colonists would move to Britain.
That Britain would lower all taxes.
That the colonies were now a free country, separate from Britain.
KARC_07_american_revolution.answer: That the colonies were now a free country, separate from Britain.

KARC_07_american_revolution.passage: Britain did not want to lose the colonies, so a war began. The colonists formed an army, and it was led by GENERAL GEORGE WASHINGTON.
KARC_07_american_revolution.question: Who led the colonists’ army during the war?

KARC_07_american_revolution.passage: After years of fighting, the colonists won the war. The thirteen colonies were no longer ruled by Britain — they became the first thirteen states of a brand-new nation.
KARC_07_american_revolution.question: What happened after the colonists won the war?
choices: The taxes were made even higher.
The colonies became the first states of a new nation.
The colonists moved away from North America.
Britain took back control of the colonies.
KARC_07_american_revolution.answer: The colonies became the first states of a new nation.

KARC_07_american_revolution.passage: Imagine a new club that makes strict rules for its members but never lets those members vote on the rules.
KARC_07_american_revolution.question: Based on the colonists’ ideas in this arc, why would the members think this is unfair?
choices: Only the newest members should vote.
Rules should always be kept secret.
People should have a say in the rules they are made to follow.
Clubs should never have any rules.
KARC_07_american_revolution.answer: People should have a say in the rules they are made to follow.
